title: TidyTuesday
text:
TidyTuesday is a weekly challenge organised by the Data Science Learning Community (DSLC). DSLC post a new real-world dataset each week for the data science community to explore, wrangle, tidy, visualise and share it with the wider community.



Understanding the data - identifying structure, variables and limitations
Exploration - identifying patterns, trends and anomalies
Visualisation - presenting insights clearly




Week 7: All agriculture production statistics in total New Zealand 1935-2024
Demonstrate how livestock categories in NZ have changed over time, when compared on a like-for-like basis.
-> View Analysis <-



Across these projects, I’ve developed and applied:

Data cleaning and transformation (tidyverse)
Exploratory data analysis
Data visualisation (ggplot2)
Reproducible workflows using Quarto
Communication of insights

title: Power BI Dashboard

My experience includes developing reporting solutions for HR and workforce analytics, with an emphasis on clarity, usability, and business impact.

title: Power BI Dashboard
text:
My Approach
When building dashboards, my focus is primarily on:

Clarity - presenting information without overwhelming the user
Usability - using intuitive layouts and filters
Relevance - aligning visuals with business questions
Consistency - clean, structured design and formatting

title: Daniella Fierro
text:
I have close to five years of experience across HR analytics, change management, and systems implementation, with a background spanning both generalist and specialist HR roles. My experience sits at the intersection of people, systems, and data, which has developed a strong interest in how these areas can work together to support better decision-making and improve organisational outcomes.
Throughout my previous roles, I worked with workforce and payroll data across multiple systems to improve reporting accuracy, streamline processes, and provide insights to support organisational initiatives and operational decisions. This included developing Power BI dashboards, analysing employee engagement survey data, and preparing workforce reporting that informed initiatives related to workforce planning, DEI, employee experience, and organisational change.
Working across both HR and data has shaped a practical and insight-driven approach to problem solving. I’m naturally curious and enjoy exploring new ways of doing things, seeking different perspectives, challenging assumptions, and continuously improving how data is used to inform outcomes. I have developed an appreciation for the importance of accuracy and attention to detail when working with data, particularly where it supports decision-making and communication across teams and stakeholders.
Motivated by this interest, I completed a Graduate Certificate in Data Science and am currently enrolled in a Master of Science in Health Data Science, pending commencement in September 2026. Alongside formal study, I continue to build my analytical capability through university and personal projects using R and Power BI, with a focus on statistical analysis, data visualisation, and reporting. I also enjoy working through complex or unfamiliar problems and see continuous learning as a core part of my development.
This site highlights a selection of my projects, experience, and ongoing learning as I continue building my skills in analytics, reporting, and data-driven problem solving.







✉️Email
🔗LinkedIn
💻GitHub

title: Daniella Fierro
text:
Professional Experience






Casual Marker - Foundations of Biostatistics
UNSW, July 2025 – August 2026



Assess student assignments, applying marking criteria consistently
Provide clear, constructive feedback to support student learning in core statistical concepts such as hypothesis testing, confidence intervals, and data interpretation.
Collaborate with course coordinators to clarify marking standards and ensure consistency across student cohorts










Business Consultant
Zero:100 Consulting, October 2023 – January 2025



Designed new policies and processes personalised to clients to improve HR practices and project delivery.
Enhanced UI/UX through WordPress.










People & Culture Officer
A-Leagues (APL), January 2022 – October 2023


HR Analytics:

Designed and managed an HR Metrics Dashboard via Power BI, integrating JavaScript customizations to align with
APL branding and providing insights on workforce demographics, turnover, and DEI initiatives.
Designed and led the complete Employee Engagement Project, from survey creation, delivery, results analysis, and recommendations. These recommendations dramatically shaped the working approach, recruitment initiatives and overall structure at the A-leagues.

Project Leadership:

Led two relocation projects, delivering within a tight timeframes and earning senior leader commendation.

HR Infrastructure:

Established automated processes for onboarding/offboarding, enhancing operational efficiency and drafted policy to support various HR workflows.
Developed a successful internship program that enabled students a professional pathway into the A-Leagues. Nalin Mastou was our first female intern, later hired and now a well-recognised sports reporter, writer and social media producer in a predominantly male dominated field.










Workforce Analyst Au/NZ
Zimmer Biomet Au/NZ, November 2019 – December 2021


Systems Deployment:

Led the payroll and time/attendance system data migrations, which included:
Align data from the internal payroll system to ADP
Accurately configure HR systems for correct data flow, set up workflows and troubleshoot bugs
Developing comprehensive training materials and conducting info/training sessions for employees and leaders

Payroll & Reporting:

Managed all things payroll for Australia and New Zealand, from processing to payment, and all pay related queries, i.e. interpretation of policy/awards
Produced reports on leave liabilities, WGEA, headcount, and compensation trends.

title: R Analysis

Welcome to the R analysis section of my quarto portfolio
This section reflects my ongoing development in R, exploring data through structured analysis, visualisation, and storytelling. It brings together projects developed through analysis, exploratory work and interactive applications.

title: Shiny
text:
Building interactive tools to make data more accessible and usable.


This section will showcase applications built using Shiny, focusing on turning analysis into interactive user experiences.
Shiny allows us to explore data dynamically, assist in answering our own questions and engage more deeply with insights, moving beyond static reports to more flexible and engaging tools.



Shiny applications will focus on interactive dashboards, scenario exploration, user-driven data filtering, simplifying complex datasets



🚧 Coming soon🚧

interactive dashboards
analytical tools
applied data exploration apps

title: Livestock Trend Analysis
text:
How have livestock categories changed over time when compared on a like-for-like basis?


Code
library(tidyverse)
library(tidytuesdayR)
library(dplyr)
library(r4np)
library(ggplot2)
library(lubridate)
library(data.table)
library(tidyr)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggplot2)
library(plotly)
library(zoo)
library(naniar)

#Load Data
tt <- read.csv("https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/refs/heads/main/data/2026/2026-02-17/dataset.csv")




Code
#Duplicate data set 
data <- tt

  ##Preview data
skimr::skim(data)

Code
glimpse(data)


Rows: 4,739
Columns: 5
$ year_ended_june <int> 1935, 1935, 1935, 1935, 1935, 1935, 1935, 1935, 1935, …
$ measure         <chr> "Total Area of Farms", "Breeding Ewes 2 Tooth and Over…
$ value           <int> 17443800, 17812400, 29077000, 15689000, 91212, 161475,…
$ value_unit      <chr> "number", "number", "number", "number", "number", "num…
$ value_label     <chr> "Hectares", "Number of sheep", "Number of sheep", "Num…


Code
#Use year to add date column in full Date format 
year <- data$year_ended_june

date <- ymd(paste0(year, "-06,30"))

data <- data |> 
  mutate(date = date)


#Categorise by industry: Agricultural/Meat

  ##Create new variable to hold 
measures_list <- data |> 
  select(date, measure, value_label, value) 


  ##Printing list of distinct values in value_label variable to further categorise by industry category
measures_list |> 
  select(value_label) |>
    distinct() |> 
  print()


                    value_label
1                      Hectares
2               Number of sheep
3                        Tonnes
4       Number of farm holdings
5              Number of cattle
6                Number of pigs
7               Number of goats
8                Number of deer
9                      Hectared
10                Square metres
11           Number of chickens
12             Number of horses
13            Number of alpacas
14             Number of llamas
15               Number of eggs
16 Number of alpacas and llamas
17                 Cubic metres
18                    Kilograms
19                       Dozens
20            Number of poultry
21          Number of livestock


Code
  ##Categorising value_labels into industry categories
measures_list <- measures_list |> 
  mutate(IndustryCategory = case_when(value_label %in% c("Hectares", "Tonnes", "Number of farm holdings", "Hectared", "Square metres", "Cubic metres", "Kilograms") ~ "Agriculture",
          TRUE ~ "Meat"))


## isolate all measures that show "Total [animal]"

totals <- measures_list |> 
  filter(str_detect(measure, "^Total")) |> 
  filter(!str_detect(measure, "value|production")) |> 
  filter(IndustryCategory == "Meat") |> 
  filter(str_detect(measure, "^")) |> 
  filter(measure %in% c(
    "Total Beef Cattle",
    "Total Dairy Cattle (including Bobby Calves)",
    "Total Sheep",
    "Total goats",
    "Total Pigs",
    "Total Deer",
    "Total poultry"
  ))

## Filter to keep only dates where ALL measures exist

totals_filtered <- totals |> 
  group_by(date) |> 
  filter(n_distinct(measure) == n_distinct(totals$measure)) |> 
  ungroup()

## Normalise values (index = 100 at first available point per measure)

totals_norm <- totals_filtered |>
  group_by(measure) |>
  arrange(date) |>
  mutate(value_index = value / first(value) * 100) |>
  ungroup()

# 3. Create annotation dataset (latest point per measure)
annotations <- totals_norm |>
  group_by(measure) |>
  slice_max(date, n = 1)

title: Livestock Trend Analysis
text:
Approach

Restricted to overlapping time periods

Indexed values to 100 at first observation

Faceted by livestock type for clarity

title: Livestock Trend Analysis
text:
Key Insight
While absolute livestock numbers differ, indexing reveals different growth patterns. Some categories in Figure 1 show steady growth, while others are more volatile or stagnant.

What Figure 1 Shows
Each line starts at 100 and shows relative change over time. This allows direct comparison of growth trends across livestock categories.


Why This Matters
This shifts the focus from size to change, helping identify which categories are growing, declining, or becoming more volatile.



Code
#| label: 
#| fig-cap: "Livestock Totals Over Time."
#| warning: false

## Visualise Livestock Totals by Year

p <- totals_norm |> 
  ggplot(aes(
    x = date, 
    y = value_index, 
    colour = measure,
    linetype = measure
  )) +
  geom_line(size = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 1.5) +
  
  #Facet for clarity 
  facet_wrap(~measure, scales = "free_y") +
  
  #Add annotations 
  geom_text(
    data = annotations, 
    aes(label = round(value_index, 1)),
    hjust = -0.2,
    size = 3,
    show.legend = FALSE
    ) +
  labs(
    title = "Livestock Totals Over Time",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Index",
    colour = "Livestock",
    linetype = "Livestock") +
  
  #Expand x-axis so labels fit
   scale_x_date(expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0.1))) +
  
  theme_minimal() +
    theme(
    legend.position = "none",
    strip.text = element_text(face = "bold")
  )


#Convert to interactive
LivestockFig <- ggplotly(p, tooltip = c("x", "y", "colour"))

LivestockFig <- LivestockFig |> 
  layout(
    hovermode = "x unified"
  )

#LivestockFig








Figure 1: Livestock Totals Over Time
